Musical artist

**Ahmad Jamal** (born **Frederick Russell Jones**; July 2, 1930 – April 16, 2023) was an American [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") pianist, composer, bandleader, and educator. For six decades, he was one of the most successful small-group leaders in jazz.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarly200179-1) He was a [National Endowment for the Arts (NEA) Jazz Master](/wiki/NEA_Jazz_Masters "NEA Jazz Masters") and won a [Lifetime Achievement Grammy](/wiki/Lifetime_Achievement_Grammy "Lifetime Achievement Grammy") for his contributions to music history.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ahmad_Jamal&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

### Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ahmad_Jamal&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early life")]

Jamal was born Frederick Russell Jones in [Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pittsburgh,_Pennsylvania "Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania"), on July 2, 1930.[[4]](#cite_note-Grove-4) He began playing piano at the age of three, when his uncle Lawrence challenged him to duplicate what he was playing.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Jamal began formal piano training at the age of seven with [Mary Cardwell Dawson](/wiki/Mary_Cardwell_Dawson "Mary Cardwell Dawson"), who he said greatly influenced him. Although Jamal is famous for his restrained playing style, he possessed an enormous piano technique from an early age and was playing [Liszt](/wiki/Liszt "Liszt") etudes in competition as young as 11 years old.[[6]](#cite_note-6) His Pittsburgh roots remained an important part of his identity ("Pittsburgh meant everything to me and it still does," he said in 2001),[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarly200179–85-7) and it was there that he was immersed in the influence of jazz artists such as [Earl Hines](/wiki/Earl_Hines "Earl Hines"), [Billy Strayhorn](/wiki/Billy_Strayhorn "Billy Strayhorn"), [Mary Lou Williams](/wiki/Mary_Lou_Williams "Mary Lou Williams"), and [Erroll Garner](/wiki/Erroll_Garner "Erroll Garner"). Jamal studied with pianist James Miller and began playing piano professionally at the age of fourteen,[[8]](#cite_note-8) at which point he was recognized as a "coming great" by the pianist [Art Tatum](/wiki/Art_Tatum "Art Tatum").[[9]](#cite_note-Waltzer-9) When asked about his practice habits by a critic from *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*, Jamal commented that, "I used to practice and practice with the door open, hoping someone would come by and discover me. I was never the practitioner in the sense of twelve hours a day, but I always thought about music. I think about music all the time."[[9]](#cite_note-Waltzer-9)

### Beginnings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ahmad_Jamal&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Beginnings")]

| External media | |
| --- | --- |
| Audio | |
| [audio icon](/wiki/File:Nuvola_apps_arts.svg) [Ahmad Jamal On Piano Jazz](https://www.npr.org/templates/story/story.php?storyId=94111325), August 29, 2008, [Piano Jazz](/wiki/Piano_Jazz "Piano Jazz")[[10]](#cite_note-npr1-10) | |
| [audio icon](/wiki/File:Nuvola_apps_arts.svg) [Eric in The Evening; Ahmad Jamal](http://openvault.wgbh.org/catalog/A_B44B3712B21F48A696DD767FFBE1C49A), interview, January 18, 1989, [Open Vault at WGBH](/wiki/WGBH_Educational_Foundation "WGBH Educational Foundation")[[12]](#cite_note-wgbhov-12) | |
| Video | |
| [video icon](/wiki/File:Nuvola_apps_kaboodle.svg) [Ahmad Jamal – Interview – "American Classical Music"](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2GvQn7LS4eY&list=PL26C87039518945EF), April 27, 2010, underyourskindvd[[11]](#cite_note-under-11) | |

Jamal began touring with George Hudson's Orchestra after graduating from [George Westinghouse High School](/wiki/George_Westinghouse_High_School_(Pittsburgh) "George Westinghouse High School (Pittsburgh)") in 1948.[[13]](#cite_note-:16-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) He then joined touring group The Four Strings, that disbanded when violinist [Joe Kennedy Jr.](/wiki/Joe_Kennedy_Jr._(violinist) "Joe Kennedy Jr. (violinist)") left.[[9]](#cite_note-Waltzer-9) In 1950 he moved to [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"),[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) performing intermittently with local musicians [Von Freeman](/wiki/Von_Freeman "Von Freeman") and [Claude McLin](/wiki/Claude_McLin "Claude McLin"),[[15]](#cite_note-15) and solo at the Palm Tavern, occasionally joined by drummer [Ike Day](/wiki/Ike_Day "Ike Day").[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Born to [Baptist](/wiki/Baptist "Baptist") parents, Jamal became interested in [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") and [Islamic culture](/wiki/Islamic_culture "Islamic culture") in Detroit, where there was a sizeable Muslim community in the 1940s and 1950s.[[17]](#cite_note-Walz-17) He converted to Islam and changed his name to Ahmad Jamal in 1950.[[18]](#cite_note-:18-18)[[13]](#cite_note-:16-13) In an interview with *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* a few years later, he said his decision to change his name stemmed from a desire to "re-establish my original name."[[17]](#cite_note-Walz-17) Shortly after his conversion to Islam, he explained to *The New York Times* that he "says Muslim prayers five times a day and arises in time to say his first prayers at 5 am. He says them in Arabic in keeping with the Muslim tradition."[[17]](#cite_note-Walz-17)

Jamal made his first records in 1951 for the [Okeh](/wiki/Okeh_Records "Okeh Records") label with The Three Strings[[19]](#cite_note-19) (which would later also be called the Ahmad Jamal Trio, although Jamal himself avoided using the term "trio"): the other members were guitarist Ray Crawford and a bassist, at different times [Eddie Calhoun](/wiki/Eddie_Calhoun "Eddie Calhoun") (1950–52), [Richard Davis](/wiki/Richard_Davis_(bassist) "Richard Davis (bassist)") (1953–54), and [Israel Crosby](/wiki/Israel_Crosby "Israel Crosby") (1954–62). The Three Strings arranged an extended engagement at Chicago's Blue Note, but leapt to fame after performing at the Embers in New York City where [John Hammond](/wiki/John_H._Hammond "John H. Hammond") saw the band play and signed them to Okeh Records. Hammond, a record producer who discovered the talents and enhanced the fame of musicians like [Benny Goodman](/wiki/Benny_Goodman "Benny Goodman"), [Billie Holiday](/wiki/Billie_Holiday "Billie Holiday"), and [Count Basie](/wiki/Count_Basie "Count Basie"), helped Jamal's trio attract critical acclaim.[[13]](#cite_note-:16-13) Jamal subsequently recorded for [Parrot](/wiki/Parrot_Records_(blues_label) "Parrot Records (blues label)") (1953–55) and [Epic](/wiki/Epic_Records "Epic Records") (1955) using the piano-guitar-bass lineup.[[20]](#cite_note-20) He recorded his first album with a drummer, [Walter Perkins](/wiki/Walter_Perkins_(musician) "Walter Perkins (musician)"), in 1956: *[Count 'Em 88](/wiki/Count_%27Em_88 "Count 'Em 88")*, which includes the influential revival of the song "[On Green Dolphin Street](/wiki/On_Green_Dolphin_Street_(song) "On Green Dolphin Street (song)")".[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### *At the Pershing: But Not for Me*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ahmad_Jamal&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: At the Pershing: But Not for Me")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Ahmad_Jamal_in_Nashville%2C_October_18%2C_2019.jpg/250px-Ahmad_Jamal_in_Nashville%2C_October_18%2C_2019.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ahmad_Jamal_in_Nashville,_October_18,_2019.jpg)

Jamal in [Nashville](/wiki/Nashville "Nashville"), October 18, 2019

The trio's sound changed significantly when Crawford was replaced with a drummer, and [Vernel Fournier](/wiki/Vernel_Fournier "Vernel Fournier") assumed this position in 1957. The group worked as the "house trio" at Chicago's Pershing Hotel.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The trio released the live album *[At the Pershing: But Not for Me](/wiki/At_the_Pershing:_But_Not_for_Me "At the Pershing: But Not for Me")*, which stayed on the Ten Best-selling charts for 108 weeks. Jamal's well-known live recording of the [Nat Simon](/wiki/Nat_Simon "Nat Simon") song "[Poinciana](/wiki/Poinciana_(song) "Poinciana (song)")", which Jamal had first recorded on *[The Piano Scene of Ahmad Jamal](/wiki/The_Piano_Scene_of_Ahmad_Jamal "The Piano Scene of Ahmad Jamal")*, was released on this album.[[23]](#cite_note-:13-23)

Perhaps Jamal's most famous recording, *[At the Pershing: But Not for Me](/wiki/At_the_Pershing:_But_Not_for_Me "At the Pershing: But Not for Me")*, was recorded at the Pershing Hotel in Chicago in 1958; it brought him an unusual level of popularity for a jazz pianist in the late 1950s and the early 1960s. The set list included various [jazz standards](/wiki/Jazz_standards "Jazz standards"), such as "[The Surrey with the Fringe on Top](/wiki/The_Surrey_with_the_Fringe_on_Top "The Surrey with the Fringe on Top")" from the [Richard Rodgers](/wiki/Richard_Rodgers "Richard Rodgers") musical *[Oklahoma!](/wiki/Oklahoma! "Oklahoma!")* and [Karl Suessdorf](/wiki/Karl_Suessdorf "Karl Suessdorf")'s "[Moonlight in Vermont](/wiki/Moonlight_in_Vermont_(song) "Moonlight in Vermont (song)")". Jamal's trio, especially through its influence on [Miles Davis](/wiki/Miles_Davis "Miles Davis"),[[24]](#cite_note-24) would come to be recognized as a seminal force in the history of jazz. Particularly evident were Jamal's unusually [minimalist](/wiki/Minimalist "Minimalist") and restrained style and his extended use of [vamps](/wiki/Ostinato#Vamp "Ostinato"), according to reviewer John Morthland.[[25]](#cite_note-25) *The New York Times* contributor [Ben Ratliff](/wiki/Ben_Ratliff "Ben Ratliff") said, in a review of the album, "If you're looking for an argument that pleasurable mainstream art can assume radical status at the same time, Jamal is your guide."[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacnie201028-26)

He attracted media coverage for his investment decisions pertaining to his "rising fortune".[[17]](#cite_note-Walz-17) In 1959, he took a tour of North Africa to explore investment options in Africa. Jamal, who was 29 at the time, said he was curious about the homeland of his ancestors, highly influenced by his conversion to the Muslim faith. He also said his religion had brought him peace of mind about his race, which accounted for his "growth in the field of music that has proved very lucrative for me."[[17]](#cite_note-Walz-17) Upon his return to the U.S. after a tour of North Africa, the financial success of *Live at the Pershing: But Not For Me* allowed Jamal to open a restaurant and club called The Alhambra in Chicago, which lasted barely one year.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-:7-28)

In 1962, the classic Jamal/Crosby/Fournier trio made its final recording, *[Ahmad Jamal at the Blackhawk](/wiki/Ahmad_Jamal_at_the_Blackhawk "Ahmad Jamal at the Blackhawk")*. Although Crosby and Fournier had started to play with [George Shearing](/wiki/George_Shearing "George Shearing"), the definitive end of the trio came with Crosby's death from a heart attack in August 1962.[[28]](#cite_note-:7-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) Jamal recorded *[Macanudo](/wiki/Macanudo_(album) "Macanudo (album)")* with a full orchestra in late 1962. He then took a brief hiatus from performing and recording.[[18]](#cite_note-:18-18)

### Return to music and *The Awakening*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ahmad_Jamal&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Return to music and The Awakening")]

In 1964, Jamal resumed performing after moving to New York and started a residency at the Village Gate nightclub.[[30]](#cite_note-:17-30) That year, he began recording a series of new trio albums with bassist [Jamil S. Nasser](/wiki/Jamil_Nasser "Jamil Nasser"), starting with *[Naked City Theme](/wiki/Naked_City_Theme "Naked City Theme")*. Jamal and Nasser continued to play together until 1972.[[31]](#cite_note-:10-31) He also joined forces with Fournier (again, 1965–1966)[[32]](#cite_note-32) and drummer [Frank Gant](/wiki/Frank_Gant "Frank Gant") (1966–77),[[33]](#cite_note-33) among others. Until 1970, he played only acoustic piano. The final album on which, for a time, he played exclusively acoustic piano in the regular sequence was *[The Awakening](/wiki/The_Awakening_(Ahmad_Jamal_album) "The Awakening (Ahmad Jamal album)")*. In the 1970s, he played electric piano as well, as on the instrumental recording of "[Suicide is Painless](/wiki/Suicide_is_Painless "Suicide is Painless")," theme song from the 1970 film *[M\*A\*S\*H](/wiki/M*A*S*H_(film) "M*A*S*H (film)")*, which was released on a 1973 reissue of the film's soundtrack album, replacing the original vocal version of the song by The Mash. Apparently, the [Rhodes piano](/wiki/Rhodes_piano "Rhodes piano") he used was a gift from someone in Switzerland. He continued to play and record throughout the 1970s and 1980s, mostly in trios with piano, bass and drums, and occasionally expanded the group to include a guitarist or a percussionist. One of his most long-standing gigs was as the band for the New Year's Eve celebrations at [Blues Alley](/wiki/Blues_Alley "Blues Alley") in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), from 1979 through the 1990s.[[34]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-34)[*[incomplete short citation](/wiki/Wikipedia:CITESHORT "Wikipedia:CITESHORT")*] Jamal also reimagined his hit song "Poinciana" several times, notably on *[Ahmad Jamal at the Top: Poinciana Revisited](/wiki/Ahmad_Jamal_at_the_Top:_Poinciana_Revisited "Ahmad Jamal at the Top: Poinciana Revisited")* (1968) and *[Digital Works](/wiki/Digital_Works "Digital Works")* (1985).

In 1986, Jamal sued critic [Leonard Feather](/wiki/Leonard_Feather "Leonard Feather") for using his former name in a publication.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### Later career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ahmad_Jamal&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Later career")]

In his 80s, Jamal continued to make numerous tours and recordings, including albums such as *Saturday Morning* (2013),[[36]](#cite_note-ford13-36) the CD/DVD release *Ahmad Jamal Featuring Yusef Lateef Live at L'Olympia* (2014), *Marseille* (2017), and *Ballades* (2019), featuring mostly solo piano.[[37]](#cite_note-:8-37) Jamal was the main mentor of jazz piano virtuosos [Hiromi Uehara](/wiki/Hiromi_Uehara "Hiromi Uehara"), known as Hiromi,[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[37]](#cite_note-:8-37) and [Shahin Novrasli](/wiki/Shahin_Novrasli "Shahin Novrasli").[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42)

## Personal life and death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ahmad_Jamal&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Personal life and death")]

Jamal was married and divorced three times. As a teenager, he married Virginia 'Maryam' Wilkins; they had one daughter, who pre-deceased him. In the early 1960s, he married Sharifah Frazier, with whom he had one daughter; they divorced in 1982. That year, he married his manager, Laura Hess-Hay. They divorced two years later but she represented him for the rest of his life.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

On April 16, 2023, Jamal died from complications of [prostate cancer](/wiki/Prostate_cancer "Prostate cancer") at home in [Ashley Falls, Massachusetts](/wiki/Ashley_Falls,_Massachusetts "Ashley Falls, Massachusetts"). He was 92.[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-:1-45)

## Style and influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ahmad_Jamal&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Style and influence")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Ahmad_jamal.jpg/250px-Ahmad_jamal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ahmad_jamal.jpg)

Jamal performing with bassist [James Cammack](/wiki/James_Cammack "James Cammack") in 2007

> "Ahmad Jamal is one of the great Zen masters of jazz piano. He plays just what is needed and nothing more... every phrase is perfect."

—[Tom Moon](/wiki/Tom_Moon "Tom Moon"), NPR musical correspondent[[46]](#cite_note-46)

Trained in both traditional jazz ("American classical music", as he preferred to call it)[[9]](#cite_note-Waltzer-9) and European classical style, Jamal was praised as one of the greatest jazz innovators over the course of his exceptionally long career. Following [bebop](/wiki/Bebop "Bebop") greats like [Charlie Parker](/wiki/Charlie_Parker "Charlie Parker") and [Dizzy Gillespie](/wiki/Dizzy_Gillespie "Dizzy Gillespie"), Jamal entered the world of jazz at a time when speed and virtuosic improvisation were central to the success of jazz musicians as artists. Jamal, however, took steps in the direction of a new movement, later coined "[cool jazz](/wiki/Cool_jazz "Cool jazz")"—an effort to move jazz in the direction of classical music. He emphasized space between notes in his musical compositions and interpretations instead of focusing on the fast-paced bebop style.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

Because of this style, Jamal was "often dismissed by jazz writers as no more than a cocktail pianist, a player so given to fluff that his work shouldn't be considered seriously in any artistic sense".[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrouch200795–99-48) [Stanley Crouch](/wiki/Stanley_Crouch "Stanley Crouch"), author of *Considering Genius*, offered a very different reaction to Jamal's music, claiming that, like the highly influential [Thelonious Monk](/wiki/Thelonious_Monk "Thelonious Monk"), Jamal was a true innovator of the jazz tradition and is second in importance in the development of jazz after 1945 only to [Charlie Parker](/wiki/Charlie_Parker "Charlie Parker").[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrouch200795-49) His unique musical style stemmed from many individual characteristics, including his use of orchestral effects and his ability to control the beat of songs. These stylistic choices resulted in a unique and new sound for the piano trio: "Through the use of space and changes of rhythm and tempo", wrote Crouch, "Jamal invented a group sound that had all the surprise and dynamic variation of an imaginatively ordered [big band](/wiki/Big_band "Big band")."[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrouch200795-49) Jamal explored the texture of [riffs](/wiki/Riffs "Riffs"), [timbres](/wiki/Timbres "Timbres"), and phrases rather than the quantity or speed of notes in any given improvisation. Speaking about Jamal, [A. B. Spellman](/wiki/A._B._Spellman "A. B. Spellman") of the [National Endowment of the Arts](/wiki/National_Endowment_of_the_Arts "National Endowment of the Arts") said: "Nobody except Thelonious Monk used space better, and nobody ever applied the artistic device of tension and release better."[[50]](#cite_note-50) These (at the time) unconventional techniques that Jamal gleaned from both traditional classical and contemporary jazz musicians helped pave the way for later jazz greats like [Bill Evans](/wiki/Bill_Evans "Bill Evans"), [Cedar Walton](/wiki/Cedar_Walton "Cedar Walton"), [McCoy Tyner](/wiki/McCoy_Tyner "McCoy Tyner"), [Herbie Hancock](/wiki/Herbie_Hancock "Herbie Hancock"), [Monty Alexander](/wiki/Monty_Alexander "Monty Alexander"), [Fred Hersch](/wiki/Fred_Hersch "Fred Hersch"), [Bill Charlap](/wiki/Bill_Charlap "Bill Charlap"), [Vijay Iyer](/wiki/Vijay_Iyer "Vijay Iyer"), and [Ethan Iverson](/wiki/Ethan_Iverson "Ethan Iverson").[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrouch200799-51)[[45]](#cite_note-:1-45)[[52]](#cite_note-Johnson,_Martin-52)[[53]](#cite_note-Hersch,_Fred-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)

Though Jamal is often overlooked by jazz critics and historians, he is frequently credited with having a great influence on [Miles Davis](/wiki/Miles_Davis "Miles Davis"). Davis is quoted as saying that he was impressed by Jamal's rhythmic sense and his "concept of space, his lightness of touch, his understatement".[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarly200179-1) Miles used to send his crew to concerts of Jamal, so they could learn to play like Miles wanted it.[[55]](#cite_note-:6-55) Jamal's contrasts (crafting melodies that included strong and mild tones, and fast and slow rhythms) were what impressed Miles.[[55]](#cite_note-:6-55) Jamal characterized what he thought Davis admired about his music as: "my discipline as opposed to my space."[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarly200180-56) Jamal and Davis became friends in the 1950s, and Davis continued to support Jamal as a fellow musician, often playing versions of Jamal's own songs ("Ahmad's Blues", "New Rhumba") until he died in 1991.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarly200179-1) In addition, in a 1960 interview, [Bill Evans](/wiki/Bill_Evans "Bill Evans") said of Jamal, "I enjoy listening to him very much." Evans emphatically rejected the "cocktail pianist" criticism of Jamal, stating, "It's a real thing he's doing."[[57]](#cite_note-57)

Jamal, speaking about his own work, said, "I like doing [ballads](/wiki/Ballad "Ballad"). They're hard to play. It takes years of living, really, to read them properly."[[58]](#cite_note-autogenerated6-58) From an early age, Jamal developed an appreciation for the lyrics of the songs he learned: "I once heard [Ben Webster](/wiki/Ben_Webster "Ben Webster") playing his heart out on a ballad. All of a sudden he stopped. I asked him, 'Why did you stop, Ben?' He said, 'I forgot the lyrics.'"[[9]](#cite_note-Waltzer-9) Jamal attributed the variety in his musical taste to the fact that he grew up in several eras: the big band era, the bebop years, and the electronic age.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarly200181-59) He said his style evolved from drawing on the techniques and music produced in these three eras. In 1985, Jamal agreed to do an interview and recording session with his fellow jazz pianist, [Marian McPartland](/wiki/Marian_McPartland "Marian McPartland") on her [NPR](/wiki/NPR "NPR") show *Piano Jazz*. Jamal, who said he rarely would play "[But Not For Me](/wiki/But_Not_for_Me_(song) "But Not for Me (song)")" due to its popularity after his 1958 recording, played an improvised version of the tune – though only after noting that he moved on to making ninety percent of his repertoire his own compositions. He said that when he grew in popularity from the *Live at the Pershing* album, he was severely criticized afterwards for not playing any of his own compositions.[[58]](#cite_note-autogenerated6-58)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Jamal_Ahmad_at_Bozar%2C_Brussels.jpg/250px-Jamal_Ahmad_at_Bozar%2C_Brussels.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jamal_Ahmad_at_Bozar,_Brussels.jpg)

Jamal at Bozar in [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), Belgium (January 2014)

In his later years, Jamal embraced the electronic influences affecting the genre of jazz. He also occasionally expanded his usual small ensemble of three to include a tenor saxophone ([George Coleman](/wiki/George_Coleman "George Coleman")) and a violin. A jazz fan interviewed by *[Down Beat](/wiki/Down_Beat "Down Beat")* magazine about Jamal in 2010 described his development as "more aggressive and improvisational these days. The word I used to use is avant garde; that might not be right. Whatever you call it, the way he plays is the essence of what jazz is."[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacnie2010-60)

Saxophonist [Ted Nash](/wiki/Ted_Nash_(saxophonist,_born_1960) "Ted Nash (saxophonist, born 1960)") described his experience with Jamal's style in an interview with *Down Beat* magazine: "The way he comped wasn't the generic way that lots of pianists play with chords in the middle of the keyboard, just filling things up. He gave lots of single line responses. He'd come back and throw things out at you, directly from what you played. It was really interesting because it made you stop, and allowed him to respond, and then you felt like playing something else – that's something I don't feel with a lot of piano players. It's really quite engaging. I guess that's another reason people focus in on him. He makes them hone in."[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacnie201031-61)

Jamal recorded with the voices of the Howard A. Roberts Chorale on *The Bright, the Blue and the Beautiful* and *Cry Young*;[[62]](#cite_note-:14-62) with vibraphonist [Gary Burton](/wiki/Gary_Burton "Gary Burton") on *In Concert*;[[63]](#cite_note-:15-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64) with brass, reeds, and strings celebrating his hometown of *Pittsburgh*;[[65]](#cite_note-:9-65) with *The Assai Quartet*;[[66]](#cite_note-66) and with tenor saxophonist [George Coleman](/wiki/George_Coleman "George Coleman") on the album *The Essence Part One*.[[67]](#cite_note-67)
